coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for trace Hg2+ detection in water sample
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2015 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Gao, Zhi-hao
Lin, Zheng-zhong
Chen, Xiao-mei
Lai, Zhu-zhi
Huang, Zhi-yong
coredata.dc:description: A facile and green method was developed to prepare amino-functionalized fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) for the detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution. The water-soluble FCDs with the diameter around 3–5nm were synthesized using anhydrous citric acid as carbon sources, NaBH4 as reducing agent and ammonia as passivation agent. The as-prepared FCDs exhibit blue emission at 440nm with quantum yield as high as 36% using quinine sulfate as reference. The fluorescence intensity of the FCDs can be quantitatively quenched by Hg2+ with high sensitivity and selectivity. The FCDs were successfully applied to the measurement of Hg2+ in water samples with a detection limit of 20nmolL−1 (3σ, n =9), indicating that the amino-functionalized FCDs probe can be potentially applied to the detection of trace Hg2+ in water samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence quenching
Mercury ion (Hg2+)
Water environment

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
Selectivity of the FCDs probe for Hg2+ detection
Effects of buffer composition and the concentrations of FCDs on Hg2+ detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by SRF for ROCS, SEM ( 201210100002 ); the Science and Technology Planning Project of Fujian Province, China ( 2012Y0052 , 2014Y0045 ); the Science and Technology Planning Project of Xiamen, China ( 3502Z20143018 ); the Foundation for Innovative Research Team of Jimei University, China ( 2010A007 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the as-prepared FCDs. Inset shows the colors of FCDs aqueous solution under visible light (1) and UV light (2). (B) Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of the FCDs. (C) Emission spectra of the FCDs at different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 390nm. (D) Fluorescent spectra of the FCDs at different pH values with the excitation wavelength of 340nm.
(A) XPS spectra of FCDs. (B–D) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (B), N1s (C) and O1s (D).

(A) Fluorescence responses of FCDs to the addition of different metal ions under acetate buffer (pH 6.0, 10mmolL−1). The concentration of each metal ion is 40μmolL−1. F 0 and F correspond to the fluorescence intensities of FCDs at λ em of 440nm excited at 340nm in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. (B) The fluorescence intensities of M+FCDs (M represents for each metal ion except for Hg2+) solutions before (gray) and after (white) mixing with Hg2+ (40.0μmolL−1).

(A) Influence of the kind of buffer on the quenching effect of Hg2+ (PBS, phosphate buffer; ACE, acetate buffer; CIT, citrate buffer). (B) Influence of FCDs concentrations on the quenching effect of Hg2+ in 10mmolL−1 of acetate buffer (pH 6.0).

(A) Emission spectra of FCDs in the presence of different concentrations of Hg2+ (from 0 to 50μmolL−1) in aqueous solution. (B) F/F 0 of FCDs (λ ex =340nm, λ em =440nm) mixed with different concentrations of Hg2+ versus the concentrations of Hg2+ in 10mmolL−1 of acetate buffer (pH 6.0). The inset plot shows the linear region of the curve (n =3).
Recovery tests of spiked Hg2+ in tap water measured with FCDs and HG-AFS, respectively (n=3).
Detection limits, quantum yield and liner ranges of different fluorescent probes for Hg2+ detection.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots-based fluorescent probe for trace Hg2+ detection in water sample

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Jimei University, Xiamen 361021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Jimei University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Microbiology and Enzyme Engineering, Xiamen 361021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fujian Provincial Key Laboratory of Food Microbiology and Enzyme Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University, Xiamen 361021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry & Chemical Engineering, Xiamen University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Jimei University, Xiamen 361021, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food and Biological Engineering, Jimei University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A facile and green method was developed to prepare amino-functionalized fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) for the detection of Hg2+ in aqueous solution. The water-soluble FCDs with the diameter around 3–5nm were synthesized using anhydrous citric acid as carbon sources, NaBH4 as reducing agent and ammonia as passivation agent. The as-prepared FCDs exhibit blue emission at 440nm with quantum yield as high as 36% using quinine sulfate as reference. The fluorescence intensity of the FCDs can be quantitatively quenched by Hg2+ with high sensitivity and selectivity. The FCDs were successfully applied to the measurement of Hg2+ in water samples with a detection limit of 20nmolL−1 (3σ, n =9), indicating that the amino-functionalized FCDs probe can be potentially applied to the detection of trace Hg2+ in water samples.

ce:section.ce:para: As one of the most hazardous and toxic heavy metals, mercury (Hg) in water environment can be readily bioaccumulated in human body through food chain, causing serious threat to human health even at very low concentrations [1–3]. Therefore, it is very important to measure trace Hg in aqueous solutions with high sensitivity and selectivity [4]. At present, many traditional detection methods, including cold-vapor atomic absorption spectrometry (CV-AAS) [5,6], cold-vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry (CV-AFS) [7,8], and inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [9,10], have been applied to measure trace Hg2+ in water samples. However, most of these methods require complicated operation and expensive instruments which limits their applications in the routine monitoring of Hg2+ [11]. It is still a great challenge to develop a rapid, simple and cost-effective method for the detection of trace Hg2+ in aqueous solutions [12].

In recent years, fluorescent carbon dots (FCDs) have gained much attention due to their low cost, good water-solubility, as well as abundant excellent optical properties [13]. As a new kind of carbon nano-particles, FCDs were first discovered in the purification procedures of single-walled carbon nanotubes in 2004 [14]. Typically, FCDs with size below 10nm display size-dependent fluorescence properties [15]. The photoluminescence from FCDs may be attributed to the presence of surface energy traps that become emissive upon stabilization as a result of surface passivation [16]. In comparison to metal quantum dots (QDs) which have serious toxicity even at relatively low concentration, the carbon nanoparticles are easy to functionalize as the fluorescent probes for bioimaging and sensing for their excellent biocompatibility [17–19]. For example, FCDs, prepared using bovine serum albumin as carbon precursor under direct acid hydrolysis, were used to sensitively and selectively detect Pb2+ in water with a detection limit of 5.05μmolL−1 [15]. Hu et al. [20] presented a new FCDs material mixed with rhodamine for the detection of Hg2+ in aqueous systems with visible light excitable fluorescence, and it was further applied for visually detecting Hg2+ in living HeLa cells. Wu et al. [21] used the rhizome of Giant Knotweed (a traditional Chinese medicine) as the carbon source to synthesize FCDs by hydrothermal method for the detection of Hg2+. The sensing principle based on the fluorescence quenching of FCDs by metal ions is presumably due to the non-radiative electron/hole recombination annihilation through effective electron transfer process [22]. The quenching mechanism of Hg2+ may be complex, both electron transfer and ion-binding, but the former effect may play a leading role [23].

In the present study, a facile and green method of synthesizing FCDs through hydrothermal reaction was developed. After reduction and passivation, the functionalized FCDs were applied to the detection of trace Hg2+ in water samples.

Anhydrous citric acid and NaBH4 were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). HgCl2, CdCl2·2.5H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, Pb(NO3)2, ZnCl2, MnSO4·H2O, MgSO4·7H2O, NiCl2·6H2O, K2Cr2O7, SrCl2·6H2O, CaCl2, HCl, NaOH, quinine sulfate and ammonium solution were purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd (Guangdong, China). The stock solution of Hg2+ (2.5mmolL−1) was prepared by dissolving HgCl2 in ultra-pure water containing 1% (v/v) HCl. The standard solution of Hg2+ (1000mgL−1, GSB 04-1729-2004) was purchased from National Center of Analysis and Testing for Nonferrous Metals and Electronic Materials (Beijing, China). All chemicals were of analytical grade, and were used without any further purification. The water used in all the experiments was purified with TKA Ultra (18.2MΩcm, Germany).

The absorption spectra were recorded with a UV-2100 spectrophotometer (Beijing LabTech, China). The Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured with a Nicolet 380 FT-IR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, US). Fluorescence spectrum measurement was performed on a Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (South East Chemicals & Instruments Ltd., Hong Kong, China). Tap water samples were determined with an AFS-640 hydride generation atomic fluorescence spectrometer (HG-AFS) (Beijing Ruili Instrumental Co., China). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were recorded with a JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan) operating at an accelerating voltage of 200kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) measurement was performed with a Phi Quantum 2000 Scanning ESCA Microprobe (Physical Electronics, US). The pH values were measured using a pH510 meter (EUTECH, Singapore).

FCDs were synthesized according to a reported method [24]. Briefly, 0.42g of anhydrous citric acid was dissolved in 10mL of deionized water, and was transferred to a stainless-steel Teflon-lined vessel, followed by hydrothermal treatment at 180°C for 5h in a drying oven (DHG-9146A, Shanghai Jing Hong Laboratory Instrument Co., Ltd.). After the reaction, the vessel was cooled to room temperature, and the reaction solution was adjusted to pH 8.0 with sodium hydroxide. Then, NaBH4 (0.25g) was added, and the solution was gently stirred for 2h followed by the addition of ammonia (535μL). The mixture was hydrothermally treated again at 180°C for another 5h. The as-prepared FCDs were finally dried under vacuum for 72h, and re-dispersed in acetate buffer (10mmolL−1, pH 6.0) at the concentration of 100gL−1 for further characterization and application.

The quantum yield (QY) of the FCDs was measured and calculated using quinine sulfate as reference. Quinine sulfate (QY=0.54 at 360nm) was dissolved in H2SO4 (0.1molL−1, refractive index (n)=1.33), while the FCDs were dissolved in acetate buffer solution (pH 6.0, n =1.33). The fluorescence spectra of the two solutions were recorded at the same excitation wavelength (λ ex =360nm). To minimize the re-absorption effects, the absorbances of both solutions were limited below 0.1 at 360nm [25]. The quantum yield was calculated by the following equation:
Q c
=
Q s
×
F c
×
A s
×
n c 2
F s
×
A c
×
n s 2
where Q is the quantum yield, F is the integrated emission intensity, n is the refractive index of the solvent, and A is the optical absorbance at 360nm measured with a UV–vis spectrophotometer. The subscript “s” and “c” refer to quinine sulfate with known QY value and FCDs, respectively.

In a typical assay, 1.5mL of FCDs solutions (90.8μgmL−1) in acetate buffer solution (10mmolL−1, pH 6.0) in centrifugal tubes (2mL) were mixed with 0.5mL of Hg2+ at different concentrations. The mixed solutions were incubated for 10min at room temperature, followed by recording the fluorescence spectra at the excitation wavelength of 340nm.

The selectivity of FCDs was examined by mixing FCDs with each of the interference ions including Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cr6+, Fe3+, Sr2+, Ca2+ and Zn2+. The fluorescence intensities of FCDs in the absence (F 0) or presence (F) of the interference ions were measured. The F/F 0 ratios of the interference ions were compared with that of Hg2+ at the same concentrations (40.0μmolL−1). For the interference testes, the FCDs solutions were respectively mixed with each of the interference ions, followed by the addition of equal dosage of Hg2+ at 40.0μmolL−1. The corresponding fluorescence intensities were recorded after mixing for 10min at room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the practicality of FCDs-based probe for the detection of Hg2+ in real samples, tap water obtained from our lab without any further pretreatment was mixed and incubated with FCDs for 10min at room temperature. Then, the fluorescence quenching intensity at λ em of 440nm was measured. The recovery experiments were also carried out by spiking the tap water with Hg2+ at three levels.

The synthesis procedure of FCDs-based probe can be divided into three steps: carbonization, reduction and passivation. In the first step, hydrothermal treatment of anhydrous citric acid at 180°C leads to the formation of carbon nanoparticles (CNPs) with many hydroxyl and carbonyl groups on their surfaces that endow CNPs with excellent water solubility. NaBH4 is a strong reducing agent that can selectively reduce carbonyl and epoxy-moieties to hydroxyl groups. Hence, NaBH4 was used in the second step to increase the amounts of hydroxyl groups on the surface, which is in favor of the passivation of the carbon dots by ammonia in the third step to enhance the photoluminescence of FCDs [24]. The photoluminescence enhancement of the N-related carbon dots may be contributed to the presence of surface energy traps in the surfaces of FCDs that become emissive upon stabilization [16].

The synthesis conditions for the FCDs were optimized. The following parameters were kept constant on optimization: 0.42g of anhydrous citric acid, 10ml of water, and hydrothermal reaction at 180°C for 5h. The synthesis conditions and the initial values for optimization are as follows: (a) the amounts of NaBH4 (0.05–0.35g) and the reduction time (1–8h) with the initial values of 2h of reduction time; (b) passivation time (3–6h), passivation temperature (120–210°C) and the molar ratio between citric acid and ammonia with the initial values of 5h of passivation time and 1:7 of molar ratio. The results were described in Fig. S1. The following experimental conditions were found to give the best results: (a) 0.25g of NaBH4 and 2h of reduction time, (b) passivation at 180°C for 5h, and 1:7 of the molar ratio between citric acid and ammonia.

The synthesis conditions for the FCDs were optimized. The following parameters were kept constant on optimization: 0.42g of anhydrous citric acid, 10ml of water, and hydrothermal reaction at 180°C for 5h. The synthesis conditions and the initial values for optimization are as follows: (a) the amounts of NaBH4 (0.05–0.35g) and the reduction time (1–8h) with the initial values of 2h of reduction time; (b) passivation time (3–6h), passivation temperature (120–210°C) and the molar ratio between citric acid and ammonia with the initial values of 5h of passivation time and 1:7 of molar ratio. The results were described in Fig. S1. The following experimental conditions were found to give the best results: (a) 0.25g of NaBH4 and 2h of reduction time, (b) passivation at 180°C for 5h, and 1:7 of the molar ratio between citric acid and ammonia.

The aqueous solution of FCDs displays clear yellow color and emits blue fluorescence under the illumination of UV light at 365nm. The fluorescent quantum yield is about 36% using quinine sulfate (QY 54% in 0.1molL−1 H2SO4, λ ex =360nm) as the reference. The strong fluorescence emissions of FCDs might be attributed to the hetero atoms introduced during the process of ammonia passivation [26]. As shown in Fig. 1 A, there are two absorption peaks at 235nm and 340nm. The peak at 235nm can be attributed to π–π* transition of the aromatic sp2 domains but has almost no fluorescence signal observed [27]. The peak at 340nm is due to the trapping of excited state energy on the FCD surfaces [26,28,29]. It should be noted that non-emissive spectrum in visible region can be observed for citric acid solution at λ ex of 340nm, revealing that the bright blue fluorescence originates from FCDs. The emission peak width, no more than 75nm (Fig. 1B), suggests a narrow particle size distribution of the as-prepared FCDs. The maximum emission wavelength (λ em) of FCDs at 440nm remains unchanged at different excitation wavelengths as shown in Fig. 1C, and the highest fluorescence intensity appears at λ ex of 340nm. The fluorescence activity of the FCDs is found to be pH-dependent as shown in Fig. 1D, in which the fluorescence intensities increase with pH values, but is stable above pH 6.0. Therefore, pH 6.0 was chosen for the subsequent experiments.

The FT-IR spectrum of FCDs is shown in Fig. 2 . The peak at 3430cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibrations of OH and NH, and the peak at 2921cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of CH [30,31]. The peaks around 2096, 1380 and 1123cm−1 are respectively accordance with the vibrations of NCN, CN and CNHC bands [24,32]. Two characteristic peaks at 1647cm−1 and 1564cm−1 are assigned to the vibrations of CO and NH, respectively, indicating the presence of amide-carbonyl (CONH) in FCDs [26,31]. According to FT-IR spectra, it can be deduced that the surfaces of FCDs should be occupied with hydrophilic groups (OH and COOH) and N-related groups (NH, NCN and CONH).

TEM image in Fig. S2 shows that FCDs are spherical with an average diameter about 3–5nm. The composition of the as-prepared FCDs characterized by XPS shows five typical peaks including C1s, N1s, O1s, Na1s and Cl2p as shown in Fig. 3 A. The presences of Na1s and Cl2p might derive from the hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide used in the pH regulation during the synthesis of FCDs. The contents of C, O and N in FCDs are 62.62% (C), 29.58% (O) and 2.01% (N), respectively. In the high-resolution region of C1s as shown in Fig. 3B, there are three peaks at 284.8, 286.4 and 288.1eV which are attributed to the CC/CC, CO and CO/CN groups, respectively [1]. Three peaks emerging at 399.6, 400.1 and 401.1eV (Fig. 3C) are assigned to the CNC, CN and NH groups, respectively [26,33]. And the spectrum of O1s (Fig. 3D) with two peaks at 531.1 and 532.6eV are derived from the CO and COH/COC groups, respectively [1]. In conclusion, the FT-IR and XPS spectra confirm that the surfaces of the as-synthesized FCDs have been functionalized with multiple oxygenated and nitrous groups.

TEM image in Fig. S2 shows that FCDs are spherical with an average diameter about 3–5nm. The composition of the as-prepared FCDs characterized by XPS shows five typical peaks including C1s, N1s, O1s, Na1s and Cl2p as shown in Fig. 3A. The presences of Na1s and Cl2p might derive from the hydrochloric acid and sodium hydroxide used in the pH regulation during the synthesis of FCDs. The contents of C, O and N in FCDs are 62.62% (C), 29.58% (O) and 2.01% (N), respectively. In the high-resolution region of C1s as shown in Fig. 3B, there are three peaks at 284.8, 286.4 and 288.1eV which are attributed to the CC/CC, CO and CO/CN groups, respectively [1]. Three peaks emerging at 399.6, 400.1 and 401.1eV (Fig. 3C) are assigned to the CNC, CN and NH groups, respectively [26,33]. And the spectrum of O1s (Fig. 3D) with two peaks at 531.1 and 532.6eV are derived from the CO and COH/COC groups, respectively [1]. In conclusion, the FT-IR and XPS spectra confirm that the surfaces of the as-synthesized FCDs have been functionalized with multiple oxygenated and nitrous groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the FCDs probe for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of FCDs as a fluorescent probe for Hg2+ detection, the fluorescence intensities of FCDs were analyzed in the presence of various metal ions including Hg2+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Pb2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Cr6+, Fe3+, Sr2+, Ca2+ and Zn2+ at the same concentrations, respectively. Results in Fig. 4 A show that no obvious decrease of fluorescence intensity is observed after the addition of all kinds of metal ions, except for Hg2+ which causes a strong fluorescence quenching. Fig. 4B shows the results of interference testes. Similarly, the fluorescence intensities of FCDs remain almost unchanged after FCDs are mixed with any one of the interference metal ions. However the significant fluorescence quench is observed on further addition of Hg2+ in each of the testing solutions. The results indicate that these metal ions do not interfere with the detection of Hg2+ except for Cr6+ and Fe3+ with a slight quenching effect on the fluorescence of FCDs. The outstanding selectivity and specificity of FCDs to Hg2+ may be explained that Hg2+ has a stronger affinity toward the electron-rich surfaces of FCDs than the other metal ions [34]. Moreover, the synthesized FCDs with hydroxyl, carboxyl and N-related groups (NH, NCN and CONH) as demonstrated by the FT-IR and XPS spectrum may present high affinity to Hg2+ to form stable chelate complexes [35]. The formed FCDs–Hg2+ complexes would facilitate non-radiative electron/hole recombination annihilation through an effective electron transfer process, leading to the distinguished fluorescence quenching effects [22,31]. In addition, the induced conversion of the functional group (CONH) from spirolactam-structure to an opened-ring amide by Hg2+ may also take important role in the fluorescence quenching effect [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of buffer composition and the concentrations of FCDs on Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: Results in Fig. 5 A show that the different buffer compositions at pH 6.0 slightly affects the initial fluorescence intensities of FCDs, but presents obvious effects on the fluorescence quenching after the addition of Hg2+. Comparing with the other buffer solutions, acetate buffer has the strongest fluorescence quenching effect. Therefore, the 10mmolL−1 of acetate buffer with pH 6.0 was chosen as the aqueous medium for Hg2+ detection.

The concentration of FCDs presents a measurable influence on the Hg2+ detection. As shown in Fig. 5B, 90.8μgmL−1 of FCDs is found to present the highest quenching efficiency (F/F 0 =0.85) for 1μmolL−1 of Hg2+.

For sensitivity study, different concentrations of Hg2+ ranging from 0 to 50μmolL−1 were mixed with FCDs under the optimized conditions. The corresponding fluorescence spectra were measured. The result shown in Fig. 6 A indicates that the fluorescence intensities obviously decrease with Hg2+ concentrations. The inset plot of Fig. 6B clearly shows the linear relationship between fluorescence intensities and Hg2+ concentrations (0.1–1.2μmolL−1) which is expressed as F/F 0
= 1.0072−0.13×[Hg2+] (R 2 =0.9936).

The excellent selectivity and high sensitivity of FCDs-based probe imply that the probe can be used for the detection of trace Hg2+ in aqueous solutions. Therefore, the FCDs were applied for the detection of Hg2+ in tap water sampled from our lab. To verify the accuracy and reliability of the analytical procedure, we also use HG-AFS to analyze the spiked tap water for comparison. The results listed in Table 1 show that the analytical result of this method is in accordance with that of the HG-AFS method. The analytical indexes of this method were also compared with those of the other reports. The comparison results listed in Table 2 clearly show that the quantum yield of the as-prepared FCDs is much higher than that of the other similar studies, indicating that our strategies of using NaBH4 and ammonia as reducing and passivating agent respectively were feasible and effective in enhancing the fluorescence of the FCDs. The high quantum yield may be beneficial to improve the sensitivity reflected by the low detection limit (20nmolL−1, 3σ, n =9) of this method.

A water-soluble amino-functionalized FCDs probe has been prepared through a facile, green and low-cost method. The preparation conditions were optimized. The diameter of the as-prepared FCDs is around 3–5nm and the quantum yield is as high as 36%. Moreover, the FCDs exhibit outstanding selectivity and sensitivity and were successfully applied for the quantitative detection of trace Hg2+ in aqueous solutions with the detection limit of 20nmolL−1. The method is rapid, simple, efficient, and accurate that can be used for the daily monitoring of trace Hg2+ in water environment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by SRF for ROCS, SEM (201210100002); the Science and Technology Planning Project of Fujian Province, China (2012Y0052, 2014Y0045); the Science and Technology Planning Project of Xiamen, China (3502Z20143018); the Foundation for Innovative Research Team of Jimei University, China (2010A007).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2015.09.032.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are the supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots: facile synthesis and application as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for detection of Hg2+ ions
Dysfunctions of the translational machinery in digestive glands of mussels exposed to mercury ions
Fluorescent sensors for measuring metal Ions in living systems
Determination of trace Hg2+ ions based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer between fluorescent brightener and CdTe quantum dots
Applicability of multisyringe chromatography coupled to cold-vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry for mercury speciation analysis
Speciation of mercury in fish samples by flow injection catalytic cold vapour atomic absorption spectrometry
Thiol-modified magnetic silica sorbent for the determination of trace mercury in environmental water samples coupled with cold vapor atomic absorption spectrometry
Determination of trace inorganic mercury in mineral water by flow injection on-line sorption preconcentration-cold vapor atomic fluorescence spectrometry
Determination of mercury in fish otoliths by cold vapor generation inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (CVG-ICP-MS)
Method to reduce the memory effect of mercury in the analysis of fish tissue using inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry
Mercury in environmental samples: speciation, artifacts and validation
Ratiometric fluorescence detection of mercuric ion based on the nanohybrid of fluorescence carbon dots and quantum dots
Ultra-sensitive and selective Hg2+ detection based on fluorescent carbon dots
Electrophoretic analysis and purification of fluorescent single-walled carbon nanotube fragments
Synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots via simple acid hydrolysis of bovine serum albumin and its potential as sensitive sensing probe for lead(II) ions
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
A fluorescent biosensor based on carbon dots-labeled oligodeoxyribonucleotide and graphene oxide for mercury(II) detection
Probing the cytotoxicity of semiconductor quantum dots
Fluorescent probes for sensing and imaging
A rhodamine-based fluorescent probe for Hg2+ and its application for biological visualization
Preparation of photoluminescent carbon nanodots by traditional Chinese medicine and application as a probe for Hg2+
Carbon nanodots as fluorescence probes for rapid, sensitive, and label-free detection of Hg2+ and biothiols in complex matrices
Use of surface-modified CdTe quantum dots as fluorescent probes in sensing mercury(II)
Tailoring surface groups of carbon quantum dots to improve photoluminescence behaviors
Phthalocyanine fluorescence at high concentration: dimers or reabsorption effect?
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based fluorescent chemosensors for sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and application of imaging in living cells
blue photoluminescence from chemically derived graphene oxide
Bandgap-like strong fluorescence in functionalized carbon nanoparticles
Nitrogen-doped photoluminescent carbon nanospheres: green, simple synthesis via hair and application as a sensor for Hg2+ ions
One-pot green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon nanoparticles as fluorescent probes for mercury ions
Surface chemistry routes to modulate the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots: from fluorescence mechanism to up-conversion bioimaging applications
Microwave-assisted rapid green synthesis of photoluminescent carbon nanodots from flour and their applications for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
Gold nanoparticle-based colorimetric and “turn-on” fluorescent probe for mercury(II) ions in aqueous solution
One-pot electrochemical synthesis of functionalized fluorescent carbon dots and their selective sensing for mercury ion
Facile Hg2+ detection in water using fluorescent self-assembled monolayers of a rhodamine-based turn-on chemodosimeter formed via a “click” reaction
Optical fiber sensor for Hg(II) based on carbon dots
Hg(II) sensing based on functionalized carbon dots obtained by direct laser ablation
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg(II) ions
Synthesis of highly luminescent graphitized carbon dots and the application in the Hg2+ detection

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Zhihao Gao obtained his B.S. degree in Food Quality and Safety from Shandong Agricultural University, China in 2013. Currently, he is a postgraduate student in Jimei University, China. His research interest includes the synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots and its applications on the detection of metal ions.

Zhengzhong Lin received his M.S. degree in Chemistry Department from Fuzhou University, China and his Ph.D. degrees in Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter, Chinese Academy of Sciences. As a teacher in Jimei University, China since 2005, he is mainly working on food analysis.

Xiaomei Chen received her B.S. and Ph.D. degree from Xiamen University in 2005 and 2011, respectively. Currently, she is working as an assistant professor in Jimei University. Her scientific interests focus on carbon and metal nanomaterials for electrochemical, electrochemiluminescence and bioanalytical applications.

Zhuzhi Lai received her M.S. degree in Fujian Institute of Research on the Structure of Matter, Chinese Academy of Sciences. As an engineer in Xiamen University, China since 2006, she is mainly working on instrument management.

Zhiyong Huang received his M.S. degree in Inorganic Chemistry from Jiangxi Normal University, China in 1989 and Ph.D. degree in Analytical Chemistry from Xiamen University in 2003. Currently, he is working as a professor in Jimei University, and his scientific interests focus on the areas of analytical chemistry, food safety, nano-materials and sample pretreatment.
